CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): Overall the paper is well organized (Intro → Method → Experiments → Conclusion + Appendix) and states its main contributions (SYMSGD, sound and probabilistically-sound combiners) early and clearly. Notation is mostly consistent and definitions (w, X, M, A, etc.) are provided with helpful equations and pseudocode, though there are minor notation/typos (e.g., the product index in Lemma A.1 looks reversed) and some spelling mistakes ("udpate", "infact", "expclitly"). Figures/tables are referred to and the algorithms are exemplified with useful pseudocode, but the provided text omits the actual figures/tables and contains a repetitive/garbled block in A.5 which hurts readability. Overall the writing is clear and accessible, with only modest editing needed to fix repetitions, minor grammatical issues, and a few notational glitches. | B(4): The paper is well organized (Intro → methods → experiments → conclusion) and states its main contribution (SYMSGD with sound/probabilistic combiners) early and clearly in the Introduction. Notation is generally consistent and definitions (e.g., SGD update, combiner M and Lemma A.1) are provided where needed, making the technical presentation easy to follow. Presentation issues are minor but present: several typographical errors and informal spellings (e.g., “udpate”, “infact”, “expclitly”), some LaTeX artifacts and a reversed-looking product notation (a∏ i=b) that may confuse readers, and a duplicated/garbled Appendix section (A.5) — figures and tables are referenced but not included in the provided text. Overall the writing is clear with only modest copy-editing and formatting fixes required.

reviewer_a.justification: Overall the paper is well organized (Intro → Method → Experiments → Conclusion + Appendix) and states its main contributions (SYMSGD, sound and probabilistically-sound combiners) early and clearly. Notation is mostly consistent and definitions (w, X, M, A, etc.) are provided with helpful equations and pseudocode, though there are minor notation/typos (e.g., the product index in Lemma A.1 looks reversed) and some spelling mistakes ("udpate", "infact", "expclitly"). Figures/tables are referred to and the algorithms are exemplified with useful pseudocode, but the provided text omits the actual figures/tables and contains a repetitive/garbled block in A.5 which hurts readability. Overall the writing is clear and accessible, with only modest editing needed to fix repetitions, minor grammatical issues, and a few notational glitches.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is well organized (Intro → methods → experiments → conclusion) and states its main contribution (SYMSGD with sound/probabilistic combiners) early and clearly in the Introduction. Notation is generally consistent and definitions (e.g., SGD update, combiner M and Lemma A.1) are provided where needed, making the technical presentation easy to follow. Presentation issues are minor but present: several typographical errors and informal spellings (e.g., “udpate”, “infact”, “expclitly”), some LaTeX artifacts and a reversed-looking product notation (a∏ i=b) that may confuse readers, and a duplicated/garbled Appendix section (A.5) — figures and tables are referenced but not included in the provided text. Overall the writing is clear with only modest copy-editing and formatting fixes required.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper proposes a clearly new approach (Symbolic SGD with explicit sound combiner matrices Ma→b = ∏(I−αXTi·Xi)) and a novel dimensionality-reduction trick (projecting M via JL-type random A and projecting N=M−I to lower variance) to preserve sequential SGD semantics in expectation. It explicitly contrasts this with prior parallel SGD schemes (HOGWILD!, ALLREDUCE, parameter servers) that do not preserve per-step semantics, and provides analytic lemmas and variance controls for the projection. The combination of exact combiner matrices for linearly dependent updates and probabilistic projection to make them practical is a non-trivial, clearly differentiated contribution that would be of interest to experts. | B(4): The paper proposes a clearly new method (SYMSGD) that constructs explicit ‘sound combiners’ (Ma→b = ∏(I − α XTi·Xi)) to recover sequential SGD semantics and then uses Johnson–Lindenstrauss random projections to produce probabilistically sound, low-dimensional combiners (Sections 2–3, Eqns.2–4 and Lemma3.1). This is differentiated from prior parallel SGD schemes (HOGWILD!, ALLREDUCE, parameter servers) which perform ad-hoc averaging or asynchronous updates, and the paper emphasizes retaining sequential semantics in expectation. The combination of symbolic combiner matrices with JL-based dimensionality reduction and the ‘‘take identity off’’ variance control is a non-trivial and notable innovation, though it builds on known tools (JL, SGD linearization), so the contribution is significant but not wholly paradigm-shifting.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper proposes a clearly new approach (Symbolic SGD with explicit sound combiner matrices Ma→b = ∏(I−αXTi·Xi)) and a novel dimensionality-reduction trick (projecting M via JL-type random A and projecting N=M−I to lower variance) to preserve sequential SGD semantics in expectation. It explicitly contrasts this with prior parallel SGD schemes (HOGWILD!, ALLREDUCE, parameter servers) that do not preserve per-step semantics, and provides analytic lemmas and variance controls for the projection. The combination of exact combiner matrices for linearly dependent updates and probabilistic projection to make them practical is a non-trivial, clearly differentiated contribution that would be of interest to experts.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper proposes a clearly new method (SYMSGD) that constructs explicit ‘sound combiners’ (Ma→b = ∏(I − α XTi·Xi)) to recover sequential SGD semantics and then uses Johnson–Lindenstrauss random projections to produce probabilistically sound, low-dimensional combiners (Sections 2–3, Eqns.2–4 and Lemma3.1). This is differentiated from prior parallel SGD schemes (HOGWILD!, ALLREDUCE, parameter servers) which perform ad-hoc averaging or asynchronous updates, and the paper emphasizes retaining sequential semantics in expectation. The combination of symbolic combiner matrices with JL-based dimensionality reduction and the ‘‘take identity off’’ variance control is a non-trivial and notable innovation, though it builds on known tools (JL, SGD linearization), so the contribution is significant but not wholly paradigm-shifting.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper's core claims are supported: Lemma A.1 correctly derives the exact combiner matrix for linear-in-parameter SGD updates and Lemma 3.1 correctly shows the JL-style projector is unbiased (IE[A A^T]=I), so the probabilistic combiner is unbiased (Sections 3 and A.1–A.2). The experimental methodology is generally sound (well-tuned Baseline, multiple competitors, hyper-parameter search; Section 4), and results demonstrate practical speedup and retained accuracy, modulo the reported initial ‘‘stutter.’’ Minor issues remain: key variance/convergence bounds are only stated and deferred to a technical report (Section 3.3), and there are no formal statistical tests of experimental variability; also the method is limited to updates linear in the model (acknowledged). These are implementation/analysis gaps but do not undermine the main technical correctness. | B(4): The core technical claims are supported: the combiner derivation (Lemma A.1) for linear-update SGD is correct by induction, and the random-projection unbiasedness (Lemma 3.1 and IE[A A^T]=I) is correctly shown so the method is unbiased in expectation. The paper acknowledges and empirically studies variance and mitigation (subtracting I and tuning combine frequency), but the detailed variance/convergence analysis is deferred to a technical report and no formal convergence-rate proof under the projection noise is given here. Experiments use a well-tuned sequential baseline and multiple competitors with hyperparameter search, but some fairness concerns remain (some HOGWILD variants run in a VM, only five-run averages and no statistical tests, and evaluation is limited to shared-memory machines). These are minor gaps that do not overturn the main technical claims.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper's core claims are supported: Lemma A.1 correctly derives the exact combiner matrix for linear-in-parameter SGD updates and Lemma 3.1 correctly shows the JL-style projector is unbiased (IE[A A^T]=I), so the probabilistic combiner is unbiased (Sections 3 and A.1–A.2). The experimental methodology is generally sound (well-tuned Baseline, multiple competitors, hyper-parameter search; Section 4), and results demonstrate practical speedup and retained accuracy, modulo the reported initial ‘‘stutter.’’ Minor issues remain: key variance/convergence bounds are only stated and deferred to a technical report (Section 3.3), and there are no formal statistical tests of experimental variability; also the method is limited to updates linear in the model (acknowledged). These are implementation/analysis gaps but do not undermine the main technical correctness.

The paper acknowledges and empirically studies variance and mitigation (subtracting I and tuning combine frequency), but the detailed variance/convergence analysis is deferred to a technical report and no formal convergence-rate proof under the projection noise is given here. Experiments use a well-tuned sequential baseline and multiple competitors with hyperparameter search, but some fairness concerns remain (some HOGWILD variants run in a VM, only five-run averages and no statistical tests, and evaluation is limited to shared-memory machines). These are minor gaps that do not overturn the main technical claims.
